data.title: Celestica: Significantly Overvalued For A No-Moat Company
data.description:
Executive Summary

A couple of weeks after my bearish Celestica (NYSE:CLS) (TSX:CLS:CA) thesis, it aged quite well as the stock price declined from around $91 to $66. There was a rebound closer to the resistance level $90 after bottoming out below $70. Everything went quite well with my bearish outlook, but the Q1 earnings release was a big positive catalyst, which made the stock rally from around $90 to $120+. Therefore, it might look that my previous thesis did not age well. I would disagree with it because Celestica is currently even more overvalued than it was in March. For a company with razor-thin margins, I think that such a generous premium over the fair value is not justified. Therefore, I downgraded Celestica's stock from SELL to STRONG SELL.

CLS Analysis

I want to start with the biggest positive catalyst that happened over the last three months, which was the Q1 earnings release. There was a double beat against consensus estimates, which the stock market met with a massive optimism. Another reason that caused a 30% rally over the last few weeks after the earnings release, was the upbeat FY2025 guidance.

There was a $150 million upgrade to the FY2025 revenue forecast, together with a more optimistic adjusted operating margin and EPS. Despite the expected improvement in core profitability metrics, the company's free cash flow outlook remained the same at $350 million. In my opinion, a 1.4% ($10.85 billion/$10.70 billion) revenue guidance is not that significant, especially taking into account that this improved revenue outlook did not affect the FCF forecast.

I do not deny that Celestica's operating margin has improved significantly over the last three years. However, it is also fair to say that the initial value of circa 3% was extremely low. Thus, despite the operating margin almost doubling over the last three years, it is currently still quite low at around 6%. Moreover, the operating margin is becoming more volatile with the growth trend becoming not so obvious. This might indicate that the operating margin expansion potential nears its peak.

Therefore, expectations of Wall Street analysts regarding Celestica's EPS growth might be exaggerated. I am saying it because the company's bottom line growth is expected to significantly outpace revenue growth over the next three fiscal years. The EPS growing significantly faster than revenue means that the company should demonstrate its significant operating margin improvements further. This might be an unfairly optimistic expectation, as we saw that Celestica's operating margin has not been that rapidly growing over the last couple of quarters.

Another indication of the assertion that Celestica's operating leverage is near its peak is the fact that the company's gross margin is not expanding. Despite quite aggressive revenue growth over the last few quarters, the gross margin has been quite stable at around 10%. Moreover, there is no clear trend of the gross margin improvement, as the metric stagnates within a quite narrow range.

Relatively low gross margin does not give much room to invest in innovation. For a company with annual sales expected at around $11 billion, around $20 million in R&D spending per quarter looks like nothing. With such low innovation spending, Celestica does not have much technological moat and pricing power. This should be another notable warning sign for investors.

The fact that Celestica currently provides a good selling opportunity is also demonstrated by insider activity. There were zero open market buys by insiders over the last twelve months, which is a screaming bearish indication. At the same time, insiders were selling quite aggressively over the last twelve months.

The assertion that insiders might see little upside is also supported by the average target price from Wall Street, which is very close to the actual current share price. Even with all the aggressive EPS expansion forecasts (which we saw previously) from Wall Street, their average target price indicates a tiny upside potential.

CLS Valuation

Such aggressive net insider selling is also highly likely to indicate significant CLS overvaluation. Let's check it by simulating a DCF model. As we mentioned earlier, the FY2025 FCF guidance is $350 million. This will be the starting point of the DCF model. My pessimistic outlook means that the DCF should be balanced out with aggressive assumptions. Therefore, I apply the FCF CAGR in line with optimistic EPS projections from consensus. A 3% growth rate into perpetuity also looks like a good choice to balance out my bearish analysis.

The discount rate is 11.56%, which is the outcome of applying CLS-related variables into the CAPM formula.

With around 22% FCF CAGR for the next five years and a very optimistic (for a no-moat company like Celestica) 3% growth rate into perpetuity, the stock is 41% overvalued.

Potential Drawbacks

There are some fundamental positives as well, which might work against the bearish opinion. Apart from a (4.77) STRONG BUY Seeking Alpha Quant rating, there is also a powerful momentum. Furthermore, the EPS revisions trend remains quite bullish. Since long-term EPS revisions from Wall Street analysts lead to adjustments to fair value calculations, potential new EPS revisions will support the bullish stance of the market.

Celestica's financial position is also a positive factor that might potentially unlock new revenue growth or profitability expansion opportunities. Celestica's total debt is not very high, below 10% of the market cap. This means that the company boasts solid financial flexibility and highly likely can easily raise several billions in debt to finance a new large project or acquisition.

Besides company-specific strengths, there might be some favorable external tailwinds as well. For instance, The U.S. tech giants might announce new aggressive AI capex spending plans, which will potentially lead to more orders for CLS and will also be met with big optimism.

The Verdict

For a company with a 10% gross margin and no apparent moat, Celestica's valuation is extremely inflated. With tiny R&D investments, it will be impossible to build a strong competitive advantage, meaning that Celestica's recent financial success is unlikely to be sustainable. As a result, downgrading this massively overvalued stock to STRONG SELL looks very logical.

data.title: Civitas Resources: Debt Maturities Should Be Manageable After Latest Bond Offering
data.description:
Civitas Resources (NYSE:CIVI) issued $750 million in new unsecured notes to pay down part of its credit facility debt and give itself plenty of liquidity to deal with its October 2026 notes.

Civitas is still aiming to pay down its net debt to under $4.5 billion by the end of 2025 and looks capable of generating over $700 million in free cash flow during the last three quarters of the year.

Civitas's value is negatively affected by weaker commodity prices, but I am still estimating its value at $54 per share in a scenario with current strip (low-$60s oil) until the end of 2026 followed by $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time.

New Unsecured Notes

Civitas is issuing $750 million in new 9.625% unsecured notes due 2033. The net proceeds from those notes are going to help pay down Civitas's credit facility debt, which was at $1.05 billion at the end of Q1 2025.

The borrowing base for Civitas's credit facility was reduced from $3.4 billion to $3.3 billion recently, but this is still well above the elected commitment of $2.5 billion.

Civitas is aiming to reduce its net debt to $4.5 billion (or less) by the end of 2025. If it achieves that goal, it will have no outstanding credit facility debt until it redeems its 2026 notes.

Civitas will have $4.85 billion in outstanding notes after issuing the $750 million in 2033 notes. It has $400 million in 5.0% unsecured notes due October 2026 that it will be redeeming next year. The low interest rate on those notes means that there is no hurry to redeem them early.

Civitas also amended its credit facility to remove the springing maturity that would have moved its credit facility maturity up to April 2026 if its October 2026 notes were still outstanding.

Civitas's next note maturity (after October 2026) is in July 2028 now. Civitas's liquidity is improved by its recent notes, but it will have approximately $390 million per year in note interest (not including its October 2026 notes).

Q1 2025 Results

Civitas's Q1 2025 production (oil and total) was within its expected ranges, albeit towards the lower-end of those ranges. It had total production of approximately 311,000 BOEPD, compared to guidance for 310,000 to 320,000 BOEPD during the quarter.

Civitas's oil production ended up at roughly 141,000 barrels per day, compared to its guidance for 140,000 to 145,000 barrels of oil per day. Civitas noted that there was a modest impact to its Q1 2025 volumes due to winter weather and windstorms, which was enough to push production towards the lower-end of expectations.

Civitas also reported $495 million in capital expenditures during the quarter, which was towards the lower end of the $490 million to $520 million guidance range. This was attributed to some DJ Basin capex shifting into Q2 2025 due to activity timing.

Civitas's cash operating expenses ended up at $11.39 per BOE during Q1 2025. This included $0.43 per BOE in short-term items such as severance costs, repair and maintenance costs due to severe weather and third-party water takeaway issues in the Permian Basin (for its Holly development). There was a contractor who was unable to fulfil their water takeaway obligations, resulting in Civitas needing to spend on alternatives. Civitas expects to recover some of those additional costs from the contractor, though.

Without those short-term items, Civitas's cash operating expenses would have been around $10.96 per BOE, still a bit above its $10.30 to $10.80 per BOE guidance range. Civitas expects significant reductions in cash operating expenses per BOE later in 2025, though.

Civitas ended up generating $171 million in adjusted free cash flow in the quarter, which allowed it to pay its base dividend as well as repurchase 1.5 million shares (for $71 million).

Cost Reductions

Civitas is aiming to reduce costs by $100+ million per year in 2026, including $40 million in savings during 2025. This is expected to be from a combination of improved capital efficiency, reduced G&A costs and reduced operating expenses.

A cost savings of $100 million per year has close to the same impact on Civitas's results as a $2 per barrel increase in oil prices.

Updated 2025 Outlook

Civitas continues to aim for approximately 330,000 BOEPD in average production during 2025, including 152,500 barrels of oil per day.

At current strip prices of roughly $64 WTI oil and $3.80 NYMEX natural gas, Civitas is projected to generate $4.708 billion in revenues for 2025. This is inclusive of $158 million of positive hedge value.

Civitas has hedges covering around 48% of its oil production during the last three quarters of the year.

I'm continuing to model Civitas's cash operating expenses and capex at the midpoint of its (unchanged) full-year guidance, despite the $40 million in expected 2025 savings.

It expects a significant decrease in cash operating expenses in the second half of the year, but those costs during the first half of the year are expected to be higher than initially anticipated, as discussed above.

Civitas would need to average roughly $9 per BOE in cash operating expenses during the second half of the year to hit the midpoint of its full-year guidance.

Civitas appears more likely to end up below the midpoint of its capex guidance than its guidance for cash operating expenses.

Civitas is now expected to generate around $884 million in free cash flow during 2025.

It had approximately $5.1 billion in net debt at the end of Q1 2025. If it puts all of its free cash flow (after its $0.50 per-share quarterly base dividend) towards debt reduction, it may end up with around $4.6 billion in net debt.

Civitas is also targeting $300 million in 2025 asset sales (likely from the DJ Basin), although that will depend on whether Civitas can get what it believes is a fair price for its assets.

With a $300 million asset sale, Civitas may be able to reduce its net debt to around $4.3 billion by the end of the year, without accounting for any additional share repurchases or variable dividends.

Notes On Valuation

Civitas's Q1 2025 results were a bit on the weaker side, although much of that was due to short-term impacts from severe weather and third-party water takeaway issues.

Lower commodity prices have a noticeable impact on Civitas's estimated value, given that it has a considerable amount of debt.

However, I believe its debt is manageable in my base case scenario, which involves current strip until the end of 2026 and then $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time.

I'd estimate Civitas's value in that scenario at $54 per share now. This assumes that it can reduce (and keep) its cash operating expenses below $10 per BOE going forward.

Conclusion

Civitas looks to be in decent shape with its various debt maturities after bolstering its liquidity with new unsecured notes due 2033. It does have a considerable amount of net debt (currently around $5.1 billion) but also looks capable of generating over $700 million in free cash flow during the last three quarters of the year (at slightly over $60 WTI oil).

I believe Civitas's shares should have considerable upside if oil can rebound to $70 by 2027 and in that scenario I estimate its value at around $54 per share. Civitas's cost-cutting measures should also help it to generate a significant amount of free cash flow at the current strip of around $60 WTI oil in 2026.

data.title: CorMedix: DefenCath In Exponential Growth Phase With Robust Market Position
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am bullish on CorMedix Inc. (NASDAQ:CRMD) due to its promising niche market and strong financials. The stock is undervalued, warranting a buy recommendation. The company’s product pipeline points to its bright outlook, as the company looks set to capitalize on the growing health expenditure in the US by exploring new products.

Despite its beta of 1.71, meaning the stock is almost 2x the market’s volatility, its attractive price return of more than 140% over the last year, beating the S&P 500 significantly, makes it a good pick. Its investment appeal is bolstered by its growth trajectory and potential.

Company Profile

CRMD was founded in 2006 with its headquarters in Berkeley Heights, New Jersey. The company is a biopharmaceutical which specializes in therapies for life-threatening diseases. The core of its business is its lead product DefenCath which was approved by the FDA in 2023. It is a novel antimicrobial catheter lock solution developed to reduce CRBSIs in adult patients with kidney failure receiving hemodialysis through CVCs. The company operates through one segment (Drug Product).

Financials: Tremendous Growth & Profitability Path

One thing that impresses me about this company is its financial progress, transitioning from a pre-revenue biotech to a profitable organization in 2025. In Q1 2025, net revenue came in at $39.1 million, driven by solid DefenCath sales. This was a significant leap compared to the 2024 FY total revenue of $43.5 million. Further, its net income for the quarter was $20.6 million translating to an EPS of $0.32, a stark contrast to its $14.5 million net loss in Q1 2024. The company’s operational efficiency was underscored by its adjusted EBITDA of $23.6 million and positive cash from operation of $19.7 million.

It is important to note that Q4 2024 was the first quarter for this company to achieve profitability with an EPS of $0.22, beating analysts’ estimates of about $0.17. This implies that the company has maintained two successive quarters of positive net income, an indication of its firm grip on its profitability route. Its commitment to maintaining profitability is reflected in its improved efficiencies, as demonstrated by its decreasing costs. For instance, IN Q1 ’25, its sales and marketing expenses went down by 29% to $4.5 million from $6.3 million the previous year.

Looking at its revised H1 2025 revised net revenue guidance of $70 million; I see the company’s confidence in its future growth, which is reflective of strong orders and a growing market penetration. This being the first and only FDA-approved antimicrobial catheter lock solution in the US, I see a strong growth potential here. It is estimated that the US has 250,000 to 500,000 CLABSI patients annually, with a mortality rate between 10% and 30%. This leads to an additional $300 million to $2.3 billion in annual healthcare burden in the US. Given that this product is in its early adoption stages and the company is expanding its reach, I anticipate an exponential growth trajectory in the coming quarters in this company’s financials, an optimism I share with other analysts.

I will end this section by estimating the company’s financials for the next three years. Guided by the 807.9% 3-year revenue CAGR and the fact that 2024 was the first year for the company to report meaningful revenue after the approval of its DefenCath in November 2023, the following are my growth assumptions:

  • 2025: I assume a 235% revenue growth, which reflects the product's high adoption in its early stages in the market.
  • 2026-2028: I assumed growth rates of 60%, 35% and 30% respectively, an indication of normalizing demand/ growth curve/market saturation. I believe the growth cycle will be this fast because this is the only FDA approved antimicrobial catheter lock solution in the US and therefore its adoption rate is likely to be high. Given these assumptions, below are my estimates of financials.

From my projection, I expect the company to grow its revenue to about $409 million by 2028 which is within the range of $335 million estimated by Seeking Alpha and the $429 estimated by Wall Street analysts as shown above. Above all, I expect the company to remain profitable, maintaining a healthy EBITDA margin. I think its exponential revenue growth and slowing growth in operating expenses will catalyze its profitability.

Market Position & Competitive Edge

CRMD serves in the biotech industry, precisely targeting the dialysis market. In the US, about 35.5 million people have kidney problems and almost 555,000 are on dialysis. The growth in kidney patients is alarming, with approximately 14% of the American adults affected. The Grand View Research projects that the US end stage renal disease market will grow by a CAGR of about 13% by 2030, further supporting the strong growth in kidney patients.

Most of the patients rely on central venous catheters, leaving them susceptible to CRBSIs. CRMD’s lead product addresses this critical unmet need of reducing the incidence of CRBSI infections.

The company has a robust market presence, securing commercial agreements that cover ~60% of the US outpatient dialysis centers. The product's high efficacy than similar products demonstrated by about 71% reduction in dialysis related infections gives it a competitive edge. Given its high market penetration in the US outpatient dialysis and its high efficacy, I believe this company has secured a strong foothold in the market. While larger players like AstraZeneca (AZN) are viewed as key competitors, this company’s niche focus (catheter lock solutions) offers a competitive advantage.

Above all, the product’s unique formulation which combines taurolidine and heparin offers a novel approach to infection prevention. It has no direct competitor currently approved for the same condition in the US market, giving it a first-mover advantage.

Valuation

Guided by the company’s strong revenue growth and outlook, I will value this stock based on PS ratio on a comparative basis. CRMD has the highest PS ratio among its peers, AVDL and CRON, and one would be quick to judge that the stock is overvalued.

However, I think the stock premium valuation is justified when we look at the companies’ respective revenue growth, where CRMD has grown exponentially due to its new product.

Based on these two metrics, I have one consideration in mind, and that is CRMD’s product life cycle. Although the current premium pricing seems justifiable, I don’t expect the company to maintain such a strong growth curve in the coming years and as such, I believe its PS ratio could fall as its revenue growth decelerates along the company’s product life cycle. As such, I expect its PS to fall within range with its peers although slightly above them because I expect CRMD to have higher growth rates than the peers despite my anticipated cooling growth. As such, I assume a forward PS of about 6x to estimate my price target for 2028.

Using my estimated revenue of $409 million by 2028 and the 6x forward PS, I project a market cap of about $2454 million. Assuming the current outstanding shares of 67.82 million, my price target for 2028 is about $36.

Risks

Market Adoption-Despite the high initial sales for DefenCath, its long-term success depends on sustained adoption by dialysis providers and health facilities. In case of a slowdown in market penetration, its revenue and the entire financial health could be severely impacted.

Stock Volatility - The company being a small-cap biotechnology is prone to price swings driven by market sentiments, and clinical updates. Its high beta implies high losses should the sentiments turn negative.

Reliance on a single product: Given that its lead product is the only commercialized offering the company has, any negative developments on it be it price weakness or regulatory hurdle could prove detrimental for this stock.

Conclusion

As I conclude, I reiterate my buy rating of this stock given its strong revenue growth and robust market position. Its lead product is in the infant stages of the market, with a massive potential considering the lack of a direct competitor. This leaves ample room for growth and value creation.

data.title: ADP National Employment Report: 37K Private Jobs Added In May
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The economic mover and shaker this week is Friday's employment report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. This monthly report contains a wealth of data for economists, the most publicized being the month-over-month change in Total Nonfarm Employment.

The forecast for the forthcoming BLS report is that 129,000 jobs were added in April. However, each month a few days before we receive the highly anticipated jobs report, ADP releases their data on new nonfarm private jobs.

The ADP employment report revealed that 37,000 nonfarm private jobs were added in May, down from 60,000 in April. This is the lowest level since March 2023 when there was a reduction of 53,000 jobs. The latest reading was lower than the expected 111,000 addition.

Here is a visualization of the two series over the past twelve months. There is no correlation between the ADP and BLS employment report.

Here is an excerpt from Wednesday's ADP report press release:

"After a strong start to the year, hiring is losing momentum. Pay growth, however, was little changed in May, holding at robust levels for both job-stayers and job-changers.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in the ADP headline number since the company's earliest published data with the new methodology in 2010. This is quite a volatile series, so we've plotted the monthly data points as dots along with a six-month moving average, which gives us a clearer sense of the trend. The latest six-month moving average is 115,000, the lowest level in ten months.

As we see in the chart above, the trend peaked in September 2015 and then went negative for the first time in late 2019, just before the NBER declared a recession start. The COVID-19 pandemic has brought employment numbers down to levels we have never seen this century. The trend reached a new high in 2021 at 875,000 and has recently dropped back to pre-pandemic levels.

ADP Employment: Industry

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into two categories: Goods Producing and Service Providing. The US is predominantly a services economy, so it comes as no surprise that Services employment has shown stronger jobs growth.

It makes sense that service-producing employment has plummeted during the pandemic for a couple of reasons - our economy is mostly supported by service-producing jobs, and during the pandemic, those same services were brought to a halt. In May, goods-producing jobs fell by 2,000 while service-providing jobs added 36,000.

The sector with the largest growth in April was Leisure & Hospitality, with a gain of 38,000. The biggest loser last month was Professional & Business Services, with a loss of 17,000 jobs.

The next chart is a linear representation of each industry's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Region

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into nine regions (census divisions). The region with the largest growth in May was the Mountain region, with a gain of 35,000 private jobs. Meanwhile, the West South Central region experienced the largest loss at -44,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each region's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Establishment Size

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into five sizes. Establishments with 50-249 employees were the only group that saw growth in May, gaining 51,000 jobs, while those with 20-49 employees experienced the largest loss at -7,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each establishment size's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 06/05/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: NIO: Don't Give Up Just Yet
data.description:
Electric vehicle start-up NIO (NYSE:NIO)(OTCPK:NIOIF) missed estimates for its first fiscal quarter on Tuesday, despite strong demand for its EVs and a ramp in ONVO deliveries, which include the popular L60 SUV. NIO is one of the largest and fastest-growing Chinese EV companies in the market, but the company suffers from high operating losses and unfortunately reported a Q/Q drop-off in its vehicle margins, a crucial performance metric for electric vehicle start-ups. Despite the short-term setback, I believe NIO is executing well against its growth targets, and the EV maker announced that it will enter more European markets in the future. Shares of NIO are unreasonably valued, in my opinion, with a price-to-revenue ratio of 0.48X, which is well below the industry group average P/S ratio.

Previous rating

I rated shares of NIO a strong buy in my last work on the EV company in March -- Massively Discounted EV Value -- as I saw a strong case for a material long-term delivery ramp. The company’s latest brand launch, ONVO, has been widely successful and although deliveries dipped at the beginning of the year due to the Chinese New Year holidays, NIO’s deliveries have picked up again. I see a favorable long-term investment setup for NIO and will continue to buy the dips along the way.

NIO missed estimates for Q1’25

NIO reported weaker-than-expected results for its bottom and top line for the first-quarter on Tuesday: the EV enterprise reported an adjusted loss of $0.42 per-share, which missed the consensus estimate by $0.05 per-share. Revenues surged $1.67B, but nonetheless missed the average prediction by $56.1M.

NIO’s delivery ramp and margin profile

NIO has had considerable success with the launch of its low-cost ONVO brand, which is making a significant delivery contribution to the EV company. NIO delivered 42,094 electric vehicles in the first-quarter, showing 40.1% growth year-over-year. This delivery boost is widely attributable to the successful launch of NIO’s low-cost ONVO brand last year in September and the popularity of the brand's first model, the L60 SUV, which appeals mostly to a younger, more price-conscious buyer demographic and is a Tesla (TSLA) Model Y competitor.

Of the 42,094 electric vehicles delivered in Q1’25, 35% of them were ONVO-branded vehicles… which is not bad for a brand that started deliveries barely eight months ago. According to NIO’s latest delivery update, the EV marker delivered 23,231 electric vehicles in the month of May, which marked a growth rate of 13.1% year-over-year.

NIO's deliveries tend to drop off in the first two months of the year as manufacturing facilities across China close for the Chinese New Year holidays. With NIO seeing strong adoption of its ONVO-models, I am optimistic that the EV maker has considerable delivery potential in 2025 and beyond.

NIO generated 12.0B Chinese Yuan ($1.7B) in revenue in the first-quarter, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 21.5%. While the EV company did reasonable well in terms of revenue growth and deliveries, given the seasonal delivery drop-off at the beginning of Q1, NIO unfortunately saw its vehicle margins decline quarter-over-year. The company’s vehicle margins contracted 2.9 PP quarter-over-quarter to 10.2%, but they still increased 1.0 PP year-over-year.

However, NIO’s rivals did much better in terms of vehicle margins. For comparison, Li Auto (LI) reported Q1 vehicle margins of 19.8% (+0.5 PP Y/Y) while XPeng (XPEV)’s vehicle margins reached 10.5% (+5.0 PP Y/Y). In terms of margins. Therefore, Li Auto still has a considerable lead on its EV rivals, with XPeng having the weakest margin profile. NIO had the second-highest vehicle margins, but was the only company in the industry group here that saw a sequential drop-off in margins, in large part due to high costs of production.

What is not looking so great is NIO’s net loss picture, as the company continues to lose a ton of money on its electric vehicle manufacturing and no major change is in sight. In the first-quarter, NIO lost as massive 6.8B Chinese Yuan ($930M) which marked an increase of 30% compared to the year-earlier period. NIO’s losses were caused mainly by higher R&D expenses as well as selling and marketing activities. Li Auto is the only electric vehicle maker in the industry group that is already widely profitable and therefore, in my opinion, the lowest-risk choice for EV investors.

Guidance for Q2’25, export strategy

NIO expects to deliver 72,000 and 75,000 electric vehicles, which implies a growth rate of between 26% and 31% year-over-year. Key to the company’s growth will be its ability to ramp up ONVO deliveries, which the company has been doing so far quite successfully, as well as international expansion in the longer term.

In a separate announcement, NIO on Tuesday said that it plans to enter markets in Europe -- including Austria, Belgium, Czech Republic, Hungary, Luxembourg, Poland, and Romania -- with its NIO-branded SUV and sedan models, such as the EL6 and the ET5. Additionally, the company seeks to penetrate new markets through its brand firefly, which is focused on high-end compact EVs. These new market entrants could be a catalyst for NIO to accelerate its delivery growth going forward and ultimately help the EV maker reduce its high operating losses.

In April, NIO said that it will bring firefly-branded EVs to 16 markets in 2025, meaning the compact EV brand is going to play a critical role in the company's expansion drive. Therefore, investors should expect not only an aggressive export push into other markets, mainly Europe, but also see a more diversified product mix in NIO's brand portfolio.

NIO’s valuation

NIO has generated significant growth in the last several years, and the company’s revenues are expected to continue to grow rapidly in the years ahead. Currently, analysts expect the EV company to generate $16.4B in revenue in FY 2026, which implies an estimated year-over-year growth rate of 29%. Between FY 2025 and FY 2030, analysts expect NIO to generate 12% average annual top-line growth, with its increasingly export-driven business model being crucial to the EV company meeting such expectations.

Shares of NIO, in part because of the company's consistent profitability issue, are trading at a big discount to the firm's longer term P/S ratio: NIO is priced at 0.48X forward (FY 2026) revenue... which represents a 70% discount to NIO's 3-year average price-to-revenue ratio.

The industry group average P/S ratio is 0.9X and implies that shares of the EV maker are valued at a 46% discount to the industry group average. As I said above, NIO has the second-highest EV margins in the EV start-up group, but the weakest earnings profile (it has the highest net losses), which is likely what caused NIO's discount to the average P/S ratio.

Under the condition that NIO can gradually lower its losses going forward, mainly through ramping up of deliveries and lowering of variable costs, I can see a path for the EV maker to revalue to the industry group average price-to-revenue ratio of ~0.9X which gives the company about 86% revaluation potential. While NIO has an inferior profitability setup and is focused on expansion, I believe in the long term the EV maker will be able to improve its earnings trajectory and therefore benefit from a higher valuation multiplier.

Risks with NIO

The biggest risk for NIO, as I see it, relates to a potential increase in competition for low-cost EV brands which could have a negative margin impact not only for NIO, but for other players in the Chinese electric vehicle market as well... especially with big companies like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) aggressively cutting costs across their entire fleet: BYD lowered costs for its EVs by up to 34% at the end of May, potentially pressuring other EV companies to follow suit in order to defend their market shares. What would change my mind about NIO is if delivery momentum for ONVO vehicles stalled, vehicle margins continued to slide, or if the EV maker failed to narrow its high operating losses.

Final thoughts

NIO did not as great as expected in the first fiscal quarter, despite solid growth in deliveries, as the EV maker is still reeling from higher losses, driving by high expenses in R&D as well as selling and marketing. The electric vehicle company also did not so great in terms of vehicle margins, which saw a sequential drop-off to 10.2%. In terms of earnings, the situation could be a little bit better as well, in my opinion, as NIO still posts significant quarterly net losses while other EV companies, like Li Auto, are already solidly profitable. However, NIO's big discount to the industry group P/S ratio compensates for this weaker earnings profile, and I see a path for a major revaluation if the EV maker continues to scale non-NIO branded EV deliveries in both China and in Europe going forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hims & Hers Health Is Building The Next Global Primary-Care Network
data.description:
Intro

Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) recently posted the kind of quarter that forces investors to redraw the company’s map. Revenue in the March period more than doubled to $586 million, net income jumped to $49.5 million, and adjusted EBITDA reached $91 million, all dramatic improvements on already rapid 2024 comps. Subscriber rolls climbed 38% to 2.4 million, and the average member now spends $84 each month, up 53% year over year, proof that the platform is monetising intensity as well as breadth. Management kept its foot on the accelerator, affirming 2025 revenue guidance of $2.3 billion to $2.4 billion while raising full-year EBITDA targets to as much as $335 million.

The share price has responded, advancing roughly 170% over the past twelve months to about $55, yet the market still values Hims at barely eight times forward sales(a figure that looked rich for a domestic niche pharmacy but now feels pedestrian for a cross-continental primary-care network in formation).

Thesis

The company’s all-cash agreement to buy London-based Zava reframes Hims as a pan-European operator overnight. Zava brings 1.3 million active patients, nearly 2.3 million annual consultations and licensed clinicians across the U.K., Germany, France and Ireland, tripling the addressable population compared with Hims’ largely U.S. footprint. Funding the deal entirely in cash while guiding to earnings accretion by 2026 underlines the balance-sheet strength that a 13% EBITDA margin already affords.

More important, the combination mates Zava’s regulatory licences and local pharmacy infrastructure with Hims’ viral marketing playbook and subscription UX. Europe’s single-payer systems reimburse many chronic therapies, making recurring subscriptions inherently stickier and lowering churn risk relative to U.S. buyers who face high deductibles. Layering Hims’ private-label generics (sporting gross margins above 80%) onto Zava’s prescription engine could lift consolidated margin by one point for every incremental $1 billion in revenue, or roughly $10 million in operating profit per point, compounding quickly under a subscription model that already shows expanding spend per member.

Europe as a Springboard

Zava is not a green-field experiment; its doctors already write in local languages and operate under U.K. Care Quality Commission rules as well as German and French telehealth statutes. Those licences are notoriously slow to obtain, so Hims shortcuts years of paperwork and capital outlay. With 85% of European consumers living within a single-payer framework that subsidises weight-management, dermatology and mental-health medicines, Zava’s reimbursement rails should translate directly into lower out-of-pocket costs for patients and higher retention for Hims.

The European push also amplifies the company’s data asset. Every consultation feeds an AI-driven intake engine that already takes in billions of signals from U.S. users; adding four new languages and drug formularies multiplies the feedback loop. Richer datasets sharpen dosage algorithms, improve adherence nudges and raise lifetime value, allowing marketing spend to stretch further in subsequent cohorts, which is an important scale benefit as Hims’ viral TikTok campaigns drift into pricier mainstream channels.

Flywheel Economics

Hims built its U.S. business by turning taboo subjects (hair loss, erectile dysfunction, anxiety) into approachable subscription kits, then upselling adjacent therapies once trust is established. Each additional product leverages an existing doctor-patient relationship, so marginal acquisition cost trends toward zero while basket size rises. Zava’s pharmacy operations in Germany and France extend that flywheel: once a user receives Wegovy or Mounjaro at British National Health Service-level prices, promoting a private-label topical minoxidil or generic SSRI becomes a matter of one extra click in the same app.

Because Hims formulates many treatments in-house, it captures gross profit that would otherwise belong to third-party brands. The gross margin was already 82% last year, higher than most consumer health peers. If local demand lets the company substitute its own generics for branded drugs inside Zava’s high-velocity prescription flow, even a modest 200-basis-point improvement in mix could add $46 million of gross profit on the midpoint of 2025 guidance. That incremental yield drops almost intact to EBITDA, given the fixed nature of the tech platform.

Product Roadmap Beyond GLP-1

Weight-loss drugs have grabbed headlines, but management stresses that metabolic care is one spoke in a much larger wheel. The U.S. Food & Drug Administration’s recent clamp-down on compounded GLP-1 suppliers exposed how regulatory shifts can whipsaw demand; Hims answered by partnering directly with Novo Nordisk to source branded Wegovy, insulating supply and margins. European markets price branded GLP-1s roughly 30% lower than in the U.S., which both widens gross margin and makes cash-pay affordability less of a hurdle for consumers.

Meanwhile, legacy franchises continue to grow. Hair-loss, mental-health and dermatology subscriptions together still account for more than half of revenue, and the company is experimenting with asynchronous video consults that could raise provider throughput by 25% without adding headcount. Management hints that European roll-outs will follow the same template, launching with familiar conditions before introducing locally tailored products such as acne treatments popular under France’s public drug plan.

Financials

First-quarter numbers paint a picture of profitable hyper-growth. Revenue soared 111%, beating the top end of earlier guidance by nearly $50 million. Net income margin reached 8.5%, a fourfold expansion from the prior-year level, while free cash flow flipped to $50 million from $12 million, helped by faster receivables turns and modest capex.

The balance sheet held $1.2 billion of cash at quarter-end against zero long-term debt, enabling the Zava purchase to be funded completely from internal resources without diluting shareholders or layering on interest expense. Even after the acquisition, pro forma leverage remains negligible, and management expects to exit 2025 with net cash thanks to guided EBITDA of up to $335 million.

Guidance implies full-year revenue growth of 57% at the midpoint, a slight deceleration from Q1’s triple-digit pace as the company laps the initial GLP-1 surge; nevertheless, adjusted EBITDA margin is projected to expand another 100 basis points to 13%, demonstrating operating leverage even while investing in Europe.

Risks

Regulation is the largest swing factor. The FDA’s stance on compounded GLP-1s showed how abruptly policy can shift, and European authorities could similarly tighten digital prescribing rules or reimbursement rates, crimping volume. Hims must also shepherd patient data across four privacy regimes, including the General Data Protection Regulation, raising compliance costs and potential fines.

Integration carries execution risk. Zava’s platform runs on technology built for European workflows; merging code bases while preserving uptime will test engineering bandwidth at a moment when U.S. growth still demands attention. Cultural integration matters too: German patients often trust local Krankenkassen more than flashy consumer brands, so transplanting Hims’ irreverent marketing voice may require nuance to avoid patient pushback.

Competition is intensifying. Teladoc (TDOC) is discounting its chronic-care bundle, and Amwell (AMWL) is pivoting into enterprise contracts with national health systems, both moves that could pressure pricing as Hims scales abroad. Finally, valuation already reflects a high-growth premium; if management under-delivers on Zava synergies or weight-loss volumes fade faster than expected, the multiple could compress sharply.

Conclusion

At roughly 7.7x trailing sales and 80x earnings, Hims screens expensive at first glance. Yet Teladoc trades at 0.6x sales and shows negative GAAP earnings, while Amwell sits near 0.4x sales with negative EBITDA, illustrating how scarce profitable growth is in telehealth. On an EV/EBITDA basis, Hims fetches about 152x, but that falls to the mid-40s on 2026 consensus once Zava accretion materializes, still far below high-growth consumer SaaS analogues.

Investors today pay a domestic tele-pharmacy multiple for a company whose addressable market has quintupled and whose profit engine remains early in its European rollout. If management executes, every incremental percentage point of margin (via private-label mix, European reimbursement or AI-driven productivity) drops almost whole to the bottom line. A 10% EBITDA margin on projected 2026 revenue of $3 billion would yield $300 million of operating profit; put even a market-average 20x multiple on that, and the equity still offers material upside from current levels. In short, Zava is an inflection, not a footnote, and the market has yet to mark the stock accordingly.

data.title: New Student Loan Debt Could Spell Trouble For Regional Management
data.description:
A recent report in Fortune Magazine painted a rather ugly picture for consumer debt in the US. Credit scores for millions of Americans are falling, in part, because of the government’s decision to restart collections on defaulted student loans. One source in the Fortune article described student loan delinquency as “severe”.

This added burden is being placed on top of a population that is already drowning in other types of debt. The Federal Reserve Bank of NY recently reported that total consumer debt has reached a record high of $18.2 trillion this year. Over three-fourths of families owe money to a financial institution, with the median debt exceeding $80,000. Credit card debt is now more common than home mortgages, and over 12% of cardholders are more than 90 days past due on their account. Auto loan delinquencies are approaching 5%. Subprime auto loans are in worse condition, with the highest delinquency rate ever recorded.

This does not appear to be a great backdrop for companies like Regional Management Corporation (NYSE:RM). The company specializes in providing installment loans to consumers with low credit scores or limited access to traditional lending sources. While the current market conditions may demonstrate a greater need for services like those offered by Regional Management, it also highlights the risk that is involved in most all of its customer transactions. I am recommending that investors stay away from new investments in RM until the US consumer can support itself on firmer footing.

Company Snapshot

Founded in South Carolina in 1987, Regional Management Corporation operates under the name Regional Finance. The company was first listed on the NYSE in 2012. Its website claims a footprint that includes over 350 branches in 19 states. Regional Finance claims that its main goal is to “help each customer find a loan that not only provides the money they need, but one that also fits their budget”. Included in the company’s offerings are fixed-rate loans that consumers can use to pay down other personal debt or pay for new indulgences like a wedding or family trip. RM also claims that its loans help its customers “build and strengthen their credit history”.

RM currently has a market cap of $261.3 million. Its revenues over the last 12 months total $577.6 million, which generated operating income of $120.1 million. At the end of the first quarter of 2025, the company reported 575,000 customer accounts, an increase of 6.4% from the same period last year. Quarterly earnings of $0.70 per share were consistent with company expectations but $0.02 higher than analysts’ projections.

Perhaps most impressive among the highlights mentioned in the company's most recent earnings presentation is the 20.2% increase in loan origination when compared to the same period in 2024. Clearly, Regional Finance offers a product that would be in high demand as more consumers struggle to make payments on their other debt obligations. Interestingly, the company was able to see a decline in it is delinquency rate of 60 basis points from Q4 2024.

The stock recently paid a quarterly dividend of $0.30 in May, an amount that has not increased since Q1, 2022. RM currently yields 4.56%, which is higher than what is on the graphic above because the stock price has fallen almost $4 per share since April 1.

The Case for Regional Management Corporation

There were certainly several good things to report in RM’s Q1 2025 earnings call. In addition to the positive earnings results mentioned earlier, the company reported a decline in seasonal liquidation compared to the same period last year. The addition of 15 new branches attracted an average of $2.2 million in new loans.

After earning $4.16 in FY 2024, the company is projected to earn $4.37 this year, a 5% increase year over year. Analysts are currently estimating earnings of $6.53 in 2026, which would give the company an earnings bump of almost 50% and a forward PE approaching 4.

What could fuel this kind of increase in earnings? The company is counting on using what it refers to as a “barbell strategy” for growth, which focuses on higher-quality auto-secured loans, as well as loans with average percentage rates of 36% or higher. Auto-secured loans have increased to 12% of the company’s total loan portfolio, after filling only 9% in the first quarter of 2024. The total value of the increase was $59 million, 37% more than the same period last year. The higher APR loans increased to 18% of the loan portfolio, up from 16% in 2024. The total increase here was also $59 million, which was a 21% increase from the same period last year.

Digging a little deeper beyond the bottom-line numbers for the company gives a mixed picture for the company’s future prospects. Two important metrics for RM, return on assets and return on equity, both fell significantly in the first quarter when compared to the previous year. ROA dropped from 3.4% to 1.5%. ROE declined from 18.4% to 7.9%. However, much of that drop can be attributed to a loan sale that occurred during Q4 of 2023 and recorded in the first quarter of last year.

The 20.2% increase in loan originations for the company in the first quarter of 2025 was the result of strong numbers from all three loan sourcing methods used by Regional Finance – Digital, Direct Mail, and Branch Originations. Digital growth was 46.1% The company has been more measured with its expectations for the remainder of 2025 with the goal of growing its loan portfolio by an additional 10%.

Of course, loan growth for the company is only valuable if the loans will be repaid. Because the company is most often dealing with consumers on the lower end of the credit rating scale, it must carefully manage the risk associated with a customer base that does not have access to more traditional lending sources and is often already stretched to their limits for repayment.

As of March 31, delinquency trends for RM were relatively stable, as can be seen on the graph below from the company’s last quarterly report. The net credit loss rate reported by the company for Q1 2025 was 12.4%, which was up from 10.8% in the previous quarter and 10.6% in Q1 2024, but the number from the first quarter of last year included the benefit of the loan sale referenced earlier.

The company is required to reserve for expected losses when loans are originated. The current allowance for credit loss rate is 10.5%, consistent with the rate in Q4 2024. The question then must be asked. Has the company planned properly for the future? Given recent events, it is hard for me to believe that all of the possible risks have been priced in. The consumer debt problem in the US was kicked into high gear last month when student loan repayments restarted.

The Problems with Consumer Debt in the US

Before the issues with student loan debt became front and center, rising credit card debt and payment delinquencies were a main focus for economists. The Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis reported in May that the percentage of consumers who were at least 30 days behind on their credit card payments was at a level not seen since the financial crisis of 2008. The share of card users who were 90 days behind was even higher.

As might be expected, the delinquency rates were impacted by consumer income. The difference between the payment rates for the lowest income ZIP codes and the highest income ZIP codes was approximately 14% for 30-day statistics and 10% for the 90-day data.

Now comes the added layer of student loan demands that may put some consumers even more at risk of defaulting on or delaying payment on their debts. It is reasonable to assume that the majority of residents in those lowest-income ZIP codes may also have the lowest amount of student loan debt. Mississippi, the poorest state in the US, has a population where less than one in four residents has attained an education beyond high school.

However, evidence I have seen shows that student loan debt appears to be affecting areas with the lowest incomes and a high number of residents who hold low credit scores. The chart below from the New York Federal Reserve shows the concentration of high delinquency rates in the South. While states like Louisiana and Mississippi, the two poorest states in the US, show rates of 30% or more, more prosperous states like Texas, Florida, and Maryland all have past due loans that make up 25 to 29.9% of the total loan pool.

Below is the graph showing the locations of Regional Finance branches in the US. With only a few exceptions, the map eerily resembles the one above. Arkansas, Kentucky, and West Virginia are the only states in dark red from the map above that do not have an RM branch within their state lines. It is reasonable to assume that many of the same people who are now facing student loan demands are the same people that are in the customer pool for Regional Finance. Interest rates on undergraduate student debt are at a level not seen in almost 15 years, and things here will likely get worse before they improve.

There are other ways to predict what the future may look like for RM. Companies that play similar roles in the finance industry may be giving signs of what it is to come. In May, Swedish lender Klarna reported a 100% increase in net losses even as its customer base and revenues are expanding. Consumer credit losses increased 17% in the first three months of this year. Both Klarna and American online lender LendingTree (TREE) are seeing a deterioration of the “buy now, pay later” market that has become a significant part of their business model. LendingTree recently reported that 41% of the recipients of these short-term loans paid late in the last year, a 7% increase from the year before.

Risk Assessment

In addition to the risks that can be tied directly to the influx of student loan bills that are now due, Regional Management Corporation is also at risk of higher loan losses should the US economy move into a recession for an extended period of time. Higher unemployment, higher interest rates, and higher inflation would all make the types of loans offered by the company more challenging to repay.

General and administrative expenses for the company rose almost 10% in the most recent quarter, in part because of new branches that have been opened in the last year. RM operates a relatively low margin business, and any additional pressures from higher expenses will impact the company's bottom line.

The Bottom Line

I spent a good bit of time asking myself if this stock deserved a sell rating or a hold. I ended up with a hold simply because the stock has already fallen over 21% year-to-date and currently trades at such low valuations. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating for RM seems to support my research. The stock receives low marks for both profitability and momentum. However, its current depressed valuation receives an “A”.

I do not see conditions changing for the company until the US consumer can become reacquainted with the demands of student loan payments and at least stabilize its behaviors with other consumer debts.

data.title: Heartland Opportunistic Value Equity Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Composite Returns*

3/31/2025

Since Inception (%) 10-Year (%) 5-Year (%) 3-Year (%) 1-Year (%) YTD (%) QTD (%)
Opportunistic Value Equity Composite (Net of Advisory Fees)** 9.92 8.77 17.36 9.10 5.87 1.25 1.25
Opportunistic Value Equity Composite (Net of Bundled Fees) 7.92 6.91 15.63 7.60 4.41 0.90 0.90
Russell 3000® Value 7.61 8.63 16.13 6.28 6.66 1.64 1.64
Source: FactSet Research Systems Inc., Russell Investment Group, and Heartland Advisors, Inc.*Yearly and quarterly returns are not annualized. The Strategy's inception date is 9/30/1999. **Shown as supplemental information. The US Dollar is the currency used to express performance. Returns are presented net of advisory fees and net of bundled fees and include the reinvestment of all income. The returns net of bundled fees were calculated by subtracting the highest applicable sponsor portion of the separately managed wrap account fee from the net of advisor fees return.

First Quarter Market Discussion

Warren Buffett famously said that market downturns don’t bother him because “it is an opportunity to increase our ownership of great companies with great management at good prices.” That is how we’re approaching this market.

The speculation that drove stocks last year abruptly ended amid growing concerns of an economic slowdown spurred, in part, by uncertainties surrounding tariffs and fiscal policy. While we are not economists and don’t approach our jobs from a macro perspective, we observe a widening range of companies highlight softening consumer and business confidence resulting from tariff uncertainty. It’s reasonable to conclude that the longer this uncertainty persists, the more damage a potential pullback in consumer spending and business investment could do to the economy.

This concern pushed stock prices lower in the first three months of the year, with the S&P 500 Index falling briefly into correction territory (defined as a drop of 10% or more) between Feb. 19 and March 13. The S&P 500 finished the quarter down 4.3%. As is often the case in a fear-driven market, small stocks were hit harder, with the Russell 2000® Index slipping around 9.5%.

At the beginning of the year, we argued that the market was overlooking potential downside risks, as there are no guarantees the new administration’s efforts to boost GDP growth would succeed. Today, the opposite may be true. Investors are so consumed with the risk of a global trade war and stagflation; they seem to be overlooking potential opportunities that market volatility is exposing. The Investors Intelligence Sentiment Index, for instance, indicates a rapid shift in investor sentiment, with bullishness dropping swiftly from exuberance to pessimism on par with the 2008-09 global financial crisis, the 2015 industrial recession, the 2020 COVID-19 shutdowns, and the 2022 interest rate spike (see the chart below).

As more businesses are being priced for a meaningful economic slowdown, we are finding opportunities to increase our exposure to parts of the market that have been adversely affected by the shifting narrative. Guided by our 10 Principles of Value Investing™, we continue to focus on attractively priced, well-managed businesses that can grow intrinsic value throughout market cycles, while avoiding areas of the market at risk of permanent capital destruction. For instance, high-yield credit spreads, a measure of excess yield required by investors to own low quality debt, have only just begun to widen after hitting a cyclical low in late February (see the chart above) thus we still see plenty of downside in the equity of highly leveraged businesses.

Attribution Analysis

Our Strategy gained 0.90% (net of bundled fees) in the first quarter, trailing the performance of the Russell 3000® Value Index, which was up 1.64%. Stock selection was mixed in the quarter, with a positive selection effect in 5 of 11 sectors, led by Utilities and Information Technology.

There was a flight to safety at the start of the year, as the best-performing sectors in the Russell 3000® Value Index were defensive areas such as Energy and Utilities while cyclicals generally lagged. However, we outperformed the benchmark in several economically sensitive areas of the market including Information Technology, Financials, and Consumer Discretionary.

Large stocks in the benchmark outperformed by more than 800 basis points, another sign of risk aversion. This proved to be a headwind for our Strategy during the quarter, as large caps comprise just 28% of our exposure, with small and midcaps representing a combined 72%. We are confident, however, that the smaller companies we own are concentrated in undervalued businesses with strong long-term prospects.

Portfolio Activity

We construct our portfolio based on individual company fundamentals rather than sectors or market-cap preference. That said, we recognize that large caps typically outperform during periods of heightened volatility. With small-cap stocks generally pricing in slower economic growth more quickly, we increased our small-cap weighting by 400 basis points to 34%. Consistent with our investment philosophy, we prioritize companies with strong balance sheets and high returns on invested capital to mitigate downside risks.

Industrials. During the quarter, we initiated a new position in Donaldson Company, Inc. (DCI), a leading manufacturer of industrial filtration systems. Filtration is a “razor & razorblade” business model, with margins significantly lower on first-fit original equipment and higher on the annuity-like revenue stream from aftermarket sales. Thanks to Donaldson’s long history of operating in niche markets, its business skews around 65% aftermarket and 35% first fit, providing a more stable revenue profile than traditional Industrial machinery suppliers.

While DCI has historically traded at a premium to its Industrial machinery peers, because of its stable and more profitable revenues streams, the stock recently faced multiple compression due to margin headwinds created by the company’s investments in the life science industry. The life science portion of the business accounts for less than 10% of revenues yet Donaldson reported an operating loss in the segment due to new product development spend that is immediately expensed through the income statement.

We believe the investment headwinds are fully priced into the stock and expect life science filtration sales to be accretive to both revenue growth and margins in the longer term. Over the next two to three years, we expect the segment’s margins to approach the corporate average, which would translate to a 10-15% lift in total earnings power for the company.

Despite softness in the ISM Manufacturing Purchasing Managers Index since late 2022, DCI has historically outperformed when Industrial activity weakens due to the consumable nature of its products. Over the past two decades, the company has traded at a median 15% premium to the Industrial sector. Yet today, the stock is at nearly a 15% discount, based on its EV/EBITDA over the next 12 months.

Information Technology. Teledyne Technologies, Inc. (TDY) has a well-managed, balanced portfolio of sensing and decision-support technologies across commercial and industrial end markets, including multiple secular growth areas spanning advanced machine vision, precision instrumentation, space and unmanned vehicles. The company’s economically sensitive customer exposure is somewhat buffered by stable markets like pollution control, water, oceanography and climate monitoring. TDY’s core markets are characterized by high barriers to entry and include niche specialized products and services unlikely to be commoditized.

In a challenging quarter for large tech stocks, Teledyne shares hit an all-time high at the start of the year, after beating consensus earnings estimates and continuing to expand profit margins despite getting little help from its challenged short-cycle end markets (where TDY has some of its highest-margin products). This helped investors imagine what earnings power could be if short-cycle markets recover.

Teledyne continues to execute secular growth opportunities while demonstrating that its commercial businesses are more economically resilient than many tech-industrial peers. Despite this strong execution and the stock’s recent performance, the shares still trade at the lower end of the relative valuation premium range that the stock has carried over the past decade. As we witness a “reality check” spreading across a number of companies that were trading at what we consider undeserved valuations, we think TDY’s prospects for strong relative performance remain bright.

Industrials. This was a challenging quarter for JB Hunt Transportation Services, Inc. (JBHT), a diversified transportation company with a focus on intermodal shipping. Customers hire Hunt to move freight in a more efficient manner than substitutes. The company owns the largest fleet of 53-foot shipping containers, which allows three ocean-freight containers to be consolidated into two Hunt containers that are moved by rail (via third party partners) and by company-owned trucks that transport containers to their final destination. Rail is the most efficient method of freight transportation, and Hunt is a key enabler of transferring semi-trailer miles onto railroads.

While the company’s fourth-quarter earnings came in slightly better than consensus estimates, management provided an uncharacteristic preview of challenging earnings expected in early 2025. Intermodal pricing is typically struck annually, and contracts agreed upon when the substituted trucking industry was operating near trough demand will carry into 2025 and keep margins depressed. While recent intermodal volumes have been strong, we believe this is largely due to inventory buildup in anticipation of tariffs, raising the likelihood of weaker demand later in 2025.

The intermodal industry appears oversupplied today; however, Hunt’s largest rail partner, Burlington Northern Santa Fe (BNSF), is constructing a major terminal expansion California. BNSF plans to have this facility complete over the next two to three years resulting in new demand that will absorb Hunt’s recent fleet growth. Despite near-term headwinds, the stock trades at around 9.6X consensus EV/EBITDA over the next 12 months, which represents a 15-year low 30% discount to the Industrial sector and is in line with the company’s long-term valuation despite a greater than 30% decline in earnings expectations since 2022. In other words, we do not expect significant multiple compression as earnings recover. JBHT operates in a cyclical industry, yet the company has grown per-share earnings at a mid-teens annual growth rate over multiple decades.

Outlook

At the beginning of the year, when investor confidence was booming and economic growth expectations were accelerating, we highlighted that sentiment is often a contrarian indicator. At the end of the first quarter, after bullishness morphed into severe bearishness, our perspective remains unchanged: selective opportunities are emerging for disciplined, patient investors. While near-term fundamentals remain challenging for many cyclical sectors, markets are generally forward-looking. We agree with Buffett that volatile times create opportunities to increase our stake in great companies at good prices. By adhering to our 10 Principles of Value Investing™, we remain committed to identifying strong management teams, durable business models, and robust balance sheets that can drive long-term intrinsic value growth.

Thank you for your continued trust and confidence.

Troy McGlone, Vice President and Portfolio Manager

Colin McWey, Vice President and Portfolio Manager

©2025 Heartland Advisors | 790 N. Water Street, Suite 1200, Milwaukee, WI 53202 | Business Office: 414-347-7777 | Financial Professionals: 888-505-5180 | Individual Investors: 800-432-7856

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

The Opportunistic Value Equity Strategy seeks to capture long-term capital appreciation by investing in companies with market capitalizations greater than $500 million. The Strategy’s flexible pursuit of value positions it as a core holding for investors.

In addition to stocks of large companies, the Opportunistic Value Equity Strategy invests in stocks of small- and mid-cap companies that are generally less liquid than large companies. The performance of these holdings generally will increase the volatility of the strategy’s returns.

Value investments are subject to the risk that their intrinsic value may not be recognized by the broad market.

Heartland Advisors, Inc. (the "Firm") claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®). The Firm is a wholly owned subsidiary of Heartland Holdings, Inc., and is registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission. For a complete list and description of Heartland Advisors composites and/or a presentation that adheres to the GIPS® standards, contact the Institutional Sales Team at Heartland Advisors, Inc. at the address listed below.

As of 3/31/2025 Donaldson Company, Inc. (DCI), JB Hunt Transport Services, Inc. (JBHT), and Teledyne Technologies, Inc. (TDY), represented 1.05%, 2.70%, and 2.27% of the Opportunistic Value Equity Composite’s net assets, respectively.

The future performance of any specific investment or strategy (including the investments discussed above) should not be assumed to be profitable or equal to past results. The performance of the holdings discussed above may have been the result of unique market circumstances that are no longer relevant. The holdings identified above do not represent all of the securities purchased, sold or recommended for the Advisor’s clients.

Statements regarding securities are not recommendations to buy or sell.

Portfolio holdings are subject to change. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to risk.

GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein.
